title: Drift
objectives: Write typed queries with the DSL and .drift files

sections.markdown: Drift is a reactive, typed persistence library for Flutter/Dart, built on SQLite. You define tables in Dart (or .drift SQL files); Drift generates type-safe query APIs and returns Streams that emit when the data changes.

Drift's strength: **full SQL power + compile-time type safety**. It scales from a small table to complex joins, and its streams make offline-first UIs trivial — the query re-emits when the underlying tables change.
Blocking the UI on heavy DB work. Use Drift's isolate support (`NativeDatabase.createInBackground`) for large queries; keep the main isolate for UI.
Prefer .drift files for complex SQL you want to review as SQL. Version your schema and write **schema tests** (Drift generates helpers) so migrations don't break on release.

flashcards.back: SQLite — with a typed Dart DSL, SQL-file support, and reactive Streams.
Drift observes affected tables and re-emits the stream when they change.

flashcards.front: How do you keep DB work off the UI thread?
flashcards.back: Use NativeDatabase.createInBackground() to run on a background isolate.

quiz.explain: It's a typed reactive layer over SQLite.

options: Any of the queried tables changes

quiz.q: For heavy DB work you should…
options: Use createInBackground() for a background isolate
quiz.explain: Keep the UI isolate free of long queries.

challenges.text: Define a Users table and watch all rows with a Stream.
Add a schema migration adding a new column with default backfill.
Write a complex join in a .drift SQL file with a generated typed API.
Move heavy queries to a background isolate; measure UI jank before/after.

interviewQuestions: How does Drift give type safety?
When to run Drift on a background isolate?
